elements.sent-2411-001: Multimodal sensing systems are increasingly prevalent in various real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-002: Most existing multimodal learning approaches heavily rely on training with a large amount of complete multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, such a setting is impractical in real-world IoT sensing applications where data is typically collected by distributed nodes with heterogeneous data modalities, and is also rarely labeled.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose MMBind, a new framework for multimodal learning on distributed and heterogeneous IoT data.
elements.sent-2411-005: The key idea of MMBind is to construct a pseudo-paired multimodal dataset for model training by binding data from disparate sources and incomplete modalities through a sufficiently descriptive shared modality.
elements.sent-2411-006: We demonstrate that data of different modalities observing similar events, even captured at different times and locations, can be effectively used for multimodal training.
elements.sent-2411-007: Moreover, we propose an adaptive multimodal learning architecture capable of training models with heterogeneous modality combinations, coupled with a weighted contrastive learning approach to handle domain shifts among disparate data.
elements.sent-2411-008: Evaluations on ten real-world multimodal datasets highlight that MMBind outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under varying data incompleteness and domain shift, and holds promise for advancing multimodal foundation model training in IoT applications\footnote{The source code for MMBind will be released once the paper is accepted.. .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Multimodal sensing systems are increasingly deployed in real-world applications such as health monitoring \cite{ouyang2024admarker, zhang2020pdlens , Augmented Reality (AR) \cite{chen2018marvel, apicharttrisorn2022breaking , localization \cite{jeong2024gdtm , and autonomous driving \cite{shi2022vips, he2023vi .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: The complexity and dynamic nature of these tasks often surpass the capabilities of a single sensor modality, requiring the integration of multiple complementary data modalities to enhance performance.
elements.sent-2411-011: Most existing multimodal learning approaches require extensive training with complete multimodal data, where each sample includes data from all sensor modalities collected simultaneously along with corresponding labels \cite{ouyang2022cosmo, liu2024focal, liu2021wavoice .}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, this paradigm is impractical in real-world IoT sensing applications where data is typically collected by distributed nodes.
elements.sent-2411-013: First, such data is usually heterogeneous and incomplete—the available multimodal data on different nodes can vary significantly, often with missing modalities or labels.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, sensor nodes deployed in homes may contain diverse sets of modalities due to deployment constraints or privacy concerns.
elements.sent-2411-015: Sensors on a specific node may also fail dynamically, resulting in changing sensor modalities at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-016: Labeling multimodal data in real-world settings is also challenging, as data from many sensors, such as IMU, are not intuitive for human annotation \cite{hu2020fine, shuai2021millieye .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Second, sensor data collected by distributed nodes is typically gathered at different times and locations, resulting in disparate data that may describe similar events.
elements.sent-2411-018: For instance, in activity recognition, a wearable worn by an outdoor subject may only collect IMU data, while an indoor node may exclusively capture skeleton data of the same activity.
elements.sent-2411-019: Given the prevalence of disparate and heterogeneous data in IoT sensing, it is vital to effectively incorporate this data for multimodal learning.
elements.figure-2411-001: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0.cm} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/MMBind_Intro.jpg} \caption{Conventional multimodal learning requires complete multimodal data. \name features a data binding approach that effectively integrates disparate data with heterogeneous modalities for multimodal learning, which also outperforms the model binding approach, where various modalities are indirectly aligned through the encoder of a shared modality. } \label{fig:intro_figure}
elements.sent-2411-020: Unfortunately, existing approaches struggle to address the unique challenges of multimodal learning with distributed and heterogeneous IoT data.
elements.sent-2411-021: One straightforward method is cross-modality generation, where missing modality data is generated from the remaining modalities \cite{wang2020cross .}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, training a robust generative model can be challenging in IoT sensing applications due to insufficient paired sensor data~\cite{karras2020training .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Recently, methods like ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind and others \cite{zhu2023languagebind, dai2024advancing aim to align different modalities to the embedding space of one central modality, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:intro_figure .}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, these model-binding approaches do not explicitly associate data modalities across different nodes and heavily depend on large amounts of data from a single central modality.
elements.sent-2411-025: Our results also indicate that they perform poorly on IoT datasets with limited data and significant domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we propose \name, a new framework for multimodal learning with distributed and heterogeneous IoT data.
elements.sent-2411-027: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:intro_figure , the key idea of \name is to leverage shared modalities to bind disparate and incomplete data collected by distributed nodes, thereby creating pseudo-paired multimodal data for model training.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreoever, the shared modality can be either sensor data or labels, as both can effectively describe the similarity of events.
elements.sent-2411-029: Instead of requiring synchronized multimodal data capturing the exact same event, we show that data of different modalities observing similar events, even captured at different times and locations, can be effectively used for multimodal training.
elements.sent-2411-030: Specifically, \name features a novel two-stage training strategy to bind distributed and heterogeneous IoT data for multimodal training, including (1) pairing incomplete data through shared modalities and (2) weighted contrastive learning with heterogeneous paired data.
elements.sent-2411-031: In the first stage, \name gathers all data samples containing the shared modality to pre-train a unimodal encoder.
elements.sent-2411-032: When the shared modality is the data label, pre-trained large language models (LLMs) can be leveraged instead.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the second stage, \name employs an adaptive multimodal learning architecture capable of training models on data with heterogeneous modality combinations.
elements.sent-2411-034: Such a design enables \name to leverage both pseudo-paired and original incomplete multimodal data for model training.
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, \name performs weighted multimodal contrastive learning based on the similarity of data pairs, thereby accommodating different contributions from pseudo-paired samples to enhance the robustness of the learned representations.
elements.sent-2411-036: As a result, \name efficiently trains multimodal joint embeddings on large volumes of incomplete data from distributed nodes, requiring only limited or even no naturally paired multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, \name supports an efficient edge implementation framework.
elements.sent-2411-038: By aggregating incomplete data samples across edge devices to train the multimodal joint embeddings, individual edge devices can subsequently fine-tune the model for specific downstream tasks, resulting in low computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-039: We extensively evaluate the performance of \name across ten different multimodal datasets, encompassing a total of nine distinct sensor modalities.
elements.sent-2411-040: The results indicate that \name achieves up to a 20\% accuracy improvement over several state-of-the-art baselines in both cross-node and cross-dataset data binding, and reduces training overhead on edge devices compared to training models from scratch using complete multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-041: In summary, we make the following key contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-042: We conduct an in-depth analysis on the impact of limited paired IoT data in multimodal learning, and show that incomplete data captured at various times and locations can be effectively used for multimodal training, as long as they describe similar events.
elements.sent-2411-043: Based on our key findings, we propose \name, a new data binding approach for multimodal learning with distributed and heterogeneous IoT data.
elements.sent-2411-044: Our evaluations on various datasets show that \name outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in multimodal learning, and holds promise for generating foundational datasets for multimodal learning in IoT.
elements.sent-2411-046: Multimodal sensing systems integrating data from multiple sensors are increasingly employed in real-world IoT applications such as speech processing ~\cite{liu2021wavoice, chen2021speech , activity recognition~\cite{liu2023activity, huang2020activity , and health monitoring~\cite{zhai2020health, samyuon2022health, ouyang2022cosmo .}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Most work in this area assumes access to full-modality and time-synchronized multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-049: Distributed sensor networks, consisting of spatially distributed sensing nodes, can vary from nodes in close proximity with overlapping fields of view \cite{samplawski2023heteroskedastic, grosswindhager2019snaploc , to deployments in entirely different environments observing distinct scenes \cite{wu2022fedhome, feng2021lte .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Training models on such spatially distributed data often employs sensor fusion algorithms \cite{jeong2024gdtm, chen2022rfcam, li2021low that integrate information from multiple perspectives, or federated learning approaches \cite{ouyang2024admarker, ouyang2023harmony that aggregate models of different nodes.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: However, incorporating data from distributed nodes with heterogeneous sensor modalities for multimodal learning remains a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-053: When performing multimodal learning in distributed multimodal sensing systems, one must contend with heterogeneous and incomplete data, where each sensor node captures a subset of the desired modalities.
elements.sent-2411-054: Several recent works have explored strategies for training with incomplete multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-055: For example, \cite{liang2022expanding, huang2021learning utilize unimodal encoders pre-trained on single-modal data to build a unified multimodal model, while \cite{wang2020cross, cai2018generation generate missing modality data from available modalities.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, these methods often struggle to effectively learn cross-modal information in IoT applications due to the scarcity of high-quality multimodal pairs.
elements.sent-2411-057: In contrast, \name leverages shared modalities across incomplete datasets to create paired data, allowing the model to learn cross-modal correlations more efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-058: Model Binding Methods such as ImageBind.
elements.sent-2411-059: Recent works such as ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind and LanguageBind~\cite{zhu2023languagebind align different modalities to the embedding space of one central modality through contrastive learning.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: All these approaches rely on model binding, where various modalities are indirectly aligned through transitivity, exposing the model only to (Central, X) modality pairs.
elements.sent-2411-061: In contrast, \name develops a data binding approach that explicitly synthesizes pairs of (Central, X, Y) data, enabling direct multimodal learning across multiple modalities.
elements.sent-2411-062: Additionally, \name utilizes the shared modality encoder only for data pairing, while ImageBind relies upon it to perform embedding space alignment across all modalities, resulting in greater susceptibility to insufficient shared modality data.
elements.sent-2411-063: Our evaluations in Section \ref{sec:evaluation further show the superiority of \name on IoT datasets with limited training data and significant domain shifts.}
elements.sent-2411-064: In this section, we evaluate multimodal learning performance with limited paired data and explore the potential of leveraging disparate and incomplete data using several real-world datasets.
elements.sent-2411-065: Here, ``paired data'' refers to full-modality data, with ``pairing'' describing the process of combining separate data samples into one with complete modalities.
elements.sent-2411-066: The key insights from these results motivate the design of \name.
elements.figure-2411-002: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0cm} \begin{minipage}{.43\linewidth} \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0.cm} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/motivation-limited-data.png} \caption{Impact of limited complete multimodal data.} \label{fig:motivation-limited-data} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{.54\linewidth} \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{-0.cm} \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{Figures/motivation-cross-dataset-IMU-label-bind.png} \caption{Adding pseudo-paired data enhances multimodal performance. } \label{fig:motivation-cross-dataset-IMU-label-bind} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-067: Obtaining full-modality and labeled multimodal data is usually impractical in real-world settings.
elements.sent-2411-068: In this section, we investigate the performance of supervised multimodal learning with different amounts of labeled paired data.
elements.sent-2411-069: Specifically, we evaluate using accelerometer, gyroscope and magnetic data from 15 subjects in the public RealWorld dataset \cite{sztyler2016body to classify five human activities (walking, sitting, standing, walking upstairs, and walking downstairs).}
elements.sent-2411-070: To simulate varying amounts of paired multimodal data, we randomly divide the dataset into training sets containing 10\%, 5\%, and 1\% of labeled data, and a testing set with 90\%, while maintaining class balance.
elements.sent-2411-071: The deep learning model used consists of five CNN layers, two GRU layers, and one fully connected layer.
elements.sent-2411-072: Each experiment is repeated five times.
elements.sent-2411-073: Figure \ref{fig:motivation-limited-data shows the performance of supervised multimodal learning with different amounts of labeled and paired data.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Even though the task is relatively simple, the accuracy is suboptimal with limited labeled data, achieving only 55.74\% with 1\% of the training data (i.e., 216 samples).
elements.sent-2411-075: Moreover, the accuracy drops significantly when the amount of training data decreases, indicating that multimodal learning heavily relies on a sufficient amount of full-modality data.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, obtaining such full-modality data is often expensive and time-consuming in practice.
elements.figure-2411-003: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0.cm} \linewidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c} Dataset Name Sensor Modality Samples 1 MotionSense Acc, Gyro 12,636 2 Shoaib Acc, Mag 4,500 3 RealWorld Acc, Gyro, Mag 21,663 (1\%\textasciitilde10\% training) \end{tabular}} \caption{By binding incomplete data from Dataset 1 and 2, we can construct additional paired multimodal data for enhancing multimodal performance on Dataset 3. } \label{table:cross_dataset}
elements.sent-2411-077: To overcome the limitation of limited paired multimodal data, we explore incorporating disparate and incomplete multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-078: As shown in Table \ref{table:cross_dataset , we utilize incomplete data from two additional datasets: (Acc, Gyro) data from the MotionSense dataset \cite{malekzadeh2019mobile , and (Acc, Mag) data from the Shoaib dataset \cite{shoaib2014fusion .}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: These two datasets cover the same five activities as the RealWorld dataset, but were collected from entirely different subjects, devices, and environments.
elements.sent-2411-080: Specifically, we create multimodal data pairs (i.e., Acc, Gyro, Mag) using samples from the MotionSense and Shoaib datasets.
elements.sent-2411-081: For each (Acc, Gyro) sample from MotionSense, we extract the Mag data from a randomly selected (Acc, Mag) sample from Shoaib of the same activity to form a new paired sample.
elements.sent-2411-082: The same process is applied to each (Acc, Mag) sample from the Shoaib dataset.
elements.sent-2411-083: This results in 17,136 new pseudo-paired samples.
elements.sent-2411-084: We then train a multimodal model using this paired data and fine-tune it with limited labeled full-modality data from the RealWorld dataset.
elements.sent-2411-085: Figure \ref{fig:motivation-cross-dataset-IMU-label-bind shows that integrating pseudo-paired data from MotionSense and Shoaib enhances performance, especially when complete multimodal data is limited.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Notably, pre-training the model with pseudo-paired samples significantly boosts the performance of fine-tuning with only 1\% of training data (77.9\%) from the RealWorld dataset, and even approaches the performance of 10\% training data (79.1\%).
elements.sent-2411-087: This case study highlights two key insights.
elements.sent-2411-088: First, binding disparate incomplete datasets proves effective for multimodal training, which can significantly reduce the effort required for collecting and annotating multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-089: Second, the effectiveness of the binding process relies on the shared information across datasets - performing binding without labels or with significant domain gaps would pose challenges.
elements.sent-2411-090: We now introduce \name, a new framework for multimodal learning with distributed and heterogeneous IoT data.
elements.sent-2411-091: We first introduce the applications and challenges, and then describe the problem formulation and system architecture.
elements.figure-2411-004: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0.cm} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/MMBind_App_Scene.jpg} \caption{\name binds heterogeneous multimodal data from distributed nodes using shared sensor modalities or labels to effectively train a full-modality model. } \label{fig:application_scene}
elements.sent-2411-092: In these applications, \name can harness heterogeneous and incomplete data collected by distributed nodes to effectively train a multimodal neural network.
elements.figure-2411-005: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0.cm} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/MMBind_Overview.png} \caption{\name consists of two stages to bind distributed and heterogeneous IoT data for multimodal training, i.e., pairing incomplete data with shared modalities and weighted contrastive learning with heterogeneous data. } \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-093: We now describe two typical application scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-094: First, multimodal data across different nodes often exhibits significant heterogeneity, with missing modalities or labels.
elements.sent-2411-095: For example, sensors available on distributed nodes can change dynamically due to deployment constraints or runtime sensor failures.
elements.sent-2411-096: As illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:application_scene , when the data is labeled, \name can pair samples from different nodes that describe the same or similar events.}
elements.sent-2411-097: For instance, in activity recognition, one node may capture only IMU data while another records skeleton data.
elements.sent-2411-098: When data labels are unavailable, \name can pair different incomplete datasets using shared sensor modalities.
elements.sent-2411-099: One node may collect IMU and audio data while another gathers IMU and skeleton data.
elements.sent-2411-100: Second, adapting multimodal neural networks to an expanded sensing suite is also challenging.
elements.sent-2411-101: For example, consider a substantial dataset of indoor scenes collected using devices with RGB cameras and depth sensors.
elements.sent-2411-102: If a new mmWave sensor is introduced, the old dataset will become incomplete in modality.
elements.sent-2411-103: In this scenario, \name can effectively make use of previously collected incomplete RGB and Depth data by pairing with the new mmWave data using shared modalities, reducing the amount of complete multimodal data to be recollected.
elements.sent-2411-104: In the following, we discuss these challenges in detail to motivate the design of \name.
elements.sent-2411-105: Generating data pairs with heterogeneous data.
elements.sent-2411-106: The first challenge is how to generate effective full-modality data from different incomplete data samples.
elements.sent-2411-107: First, sensor data in IoT applications is usually highly heterogeneous, with varying dimensions and data patterns, making it nontrivial to quantify the similarity of the shared modality for data pairing.
elements.sent-2411-108: Second, when multiple overlapping modalities are present, selecting the optimal common modality becomes crucial for enhancing the effectiveness of data pairing.
elements.sent-2411-110: Another challenge is to address the varying degrees of domain gap among disparate and incomplete data.
elements.sent-2411-111: Data collected by distributed nodes can vary significantly in terms of environments or observed events, introducing substantial domain gaps.
elements.sent-2411-112: This variability increases the difficulty of accurately quantifying sample similarity and complicates the data pairing process.
elements.sent-2411-113: Furthermore, multimodal learning with improperly paired data samples can result in degraded model performance.
elements.sent-2411-114: Without loss of generality, suppose there are two incomplete multimodal datasets collected by different nodes, i.e., $D_A$ by Node A and $D_B$ by Node B.
elements.sent-2411-115: The data samples in $D_A$ and $D_B$ contain up to $M$ ($M \ge 2$) different modalities in total. \begin{equation} D_A : \{s: X_A\}, \quad X_A = \{ m_i \mid m_i \in M_A \}, D_B : \{s: X_B\}, \quad X_B = \{ m_i \mid m_i \in M_B \}. \end{equation} Here $\mathbf{X _k = \{ \mathbf{m _i \mid \mathbf{m _i \in \mathcal{M _k \}$ ($k$ is either A or B) contains $|\mathcal{M _k|$ different modalities, with $\mathbf{m _i$ denoting sensor data or data labels.}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} D_A : \{s: X_A\}, \quad X_A = \{ m_i \mid m_i \in M_A \}, D_B : \{s: X_B\}, \quad X_B = \{ m_i \mid m_i \in M_B \}. \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-116: We define $D_A$ and $D_B$ as two disparate datasets characterized by the following properties: (1) Their data is collected by different nodes at different times and locations; (2) Their data samples are incomplete in modality, i.e., $1 \le |\mathcal{M _A| < M$, and $1 \le |\mathcal{M _B| < M$; (3) The samples in $D_A$ and $D_B$ share common modalities $\{\mathbf{m_s}\}$ such that $ \mathcal{M _A \cap \mathcal{M _B = \{\mathbf{m_s}\} \neq \emptyset.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: In the case of label binding, given $D_A: \{s: (\text{Gyro , \text{Label )\}$ and $D_B: \{s: (\text{Mag , \text{Label )\}$, \name can bind $D_A$ and $D_B$ with the shared modality $\{\mathbf{m_s : \text{Label}\}$ to train a multimodal model with inputs of $(\text{Gyro , \text{Mag )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: We term this overall approach as data binding, where we explicitly create full-modality samples $\{\mathbf{m _1, \mathbf{m _2, \mathbf{m _s \}$, each with all $M$ modalities, to jointly train encoders $f_{enc_{m_1 }$, $f_{enc_{m_2 }$, $f_{enc_{m_s }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: In comparison, prior model binding works such as ImageBind~\cite{girdhar2023imagebind align encoders of different modalities to the shared modality encoder's ($f_{enc_{m_s }$) output embedding space based on the original incomplete data samples.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The design of \name is motivated by the key insight from Section \ref{sec:motivate_incomplete that distributed data from different modalities, captured at various times and locations, can be leveraged to enhance multimodal training if they describe similar events.}
elements.sent-2411-122: Therefore, our key idea is to bind incomplete multimodal data based on the shared modality, and create pseudo-paired multimodal data samples to learn multimodal joint embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-123: Figure \ref{fig:overview shows the overall system architecture of \name.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Specifically, \name features a new two-stage training strategy to bind distributed and heterogeneous IoT data for multimodal training, including pairing incomplete data with shared modalities and weighted contrastive learning with heterogeneous paired data.
elements.sent-2411-125: In the first stage, \name gathers all samples containing the shared modality and trains the corresponding unimodal encoder.
elements.sent-2411-126: This shared modality can be either sensor data or labels, as both can describe the similarity of events captured by distributed nodes.
elements.sent-2411-127: When the shared modality is a data label, pre-trained large language models (LLMs) can be leveraged to measure similarity of samples.
elements.sent-2411-128: Then, \name utilizes the feature similarity of the shared modality to construct pseudo-paired multimodal data samples by matching the most similar samples across different incomplete datasets.
elements.sent-2411-129: Finally, each pseudo-paired multimodal sample will be associated with a corresponding similarity measurement.
elements.sent-2411-130: In the second stage, \name employs an adaptive multimodal learning architecture capable of training models with heterogeneous modality combinations, by representing missing modalities with dummy inputs.
elements.sent-2411-131: Therefore, it can integrate both pseudo-paired and original incomplete multimodal data into the training process.
elements.sent-2411-132: Furthermore, to account for imperfect pairing caused by domain shifts among disparate data sources, \name introduces weighted contrastive learning on the pseudo-paired multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-133: As a result, \name efficiently learns multimodal joint embeddings from distributed and heterogeneous IoT data, requiring only limited or even no naturally paired multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-134: We propose a novel two-stage framework to leverage distributed and heterogeneous IoT data for multimodal learning, namely pairing incomplete data with shared modalities and weighted multimodal contrastive learning with paired data.
elements.subsection-2411-006: Pairing Incomplete Data with Shared Modality
elements.sent-2411-135: In the first stage, \name aims to pair distributed data with different modalities based on the similarity of their shared modality.
elements.sent-2411-136: We consider labels as nothing more than a natural language modality representing data in a highly compressed manner.
elements.sent-2411-137: Therefore, the shared modality can be either sensor data or labels, as both can describe the similarity of events captured by distributed nodes.
elements.sent-2411-138: Without loss of generality, suppose $D_A : \{s: (\mathbf{m_1 , \mathbf{m_s )\}$ and $D_B : \{s: (\mathbf{m_2 , \mathbf{m_s )\}$ denotes two disparate datasets, with which we aim to train multimodal models for processing three modalities $(\mathbf{m_1 , \mathbf{m_2 , \mathbf{m_s )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-139: We propose to measure the similarity between samples in $D_A$ and $D_B$ by comparing the shared modality $\{\mathbf{m_s}\}$.
elements.sent-2411-140: Since either $(\mathbf{m_1 , \mathbf{m_s )$ or $(\mathbf{m_2 , \mathbf{m_s )$ consists of samples from different modalities collected at the same time and location, the similarity across modality $\mathbf{m_s}$ indicates a strong correlation between their associated samples of modality $\mathbf{m_1}$ and $\mathbf{m_2}$.}}}}
elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Encoder Training of the Shared Modality.
elements.sent-2411-141: Instead of directly comparing the high-dimensional raw sensor data, we propose to use a feature encoder to compress the shared modality data into a latent space and measure the similarity of the resulting embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-142: Recent works \cite{connor2021vae, kirchoff2024salsa, xurebar show that properly trained autoencoders exhibit structured latent spaces, where semantically similar samples are grouped close together while dissimilar samples are further apart.}
elements.sent-2411-143: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:measure_sim , \name first takes the union of all data from the shared modality, i.e., $\mathbf{m_s}$ from $D_A$ and $D_B$, to train a unimodal encoder of $\mathbf{m_s}$.}
elements.sent-2411-144: Specifically, we train the unimodal encoder in a self-supervised manner using an autoencoder.
elements.sent-2411-145: The autoencoder consists of an encoder $f_{enc_s (\cdot)$ that compresses the data into a smaller latent space, followed by a decoder $f_{dec_s (\cdot)$ that reconstructs the original input data from the latent embedding.}}
elements.sent-2411-146: The reconstruction loss is defined as the L2 distance between the decoder's output and the original input data.
elements.sent-2411-147: Suppose the reconstructed data is $\mathbf{m' _s = f_{dec_s (f_{enc_s (\mathbf{m_s ))$, then the reconstruction loss is given by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{reconstruct} = || \mathbf{m'}^j_s - \mathbf{m}^j_s ||^2, j=1, ..., |D_A|+|D_B| \label{eq:reconstruct_loss}\end{equation} Through the training process, the encoder learns to compress semantically essential features of the input data into a smaller latent space, while effectively filtering out irrelevant features.}}}}
elements.figure-2411-006: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0.cm} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/MMBind_Step1.png} \caption{Pairing incomplete data with the shared modality. We train an encoder on the shared modality data through self-supervised reconstruction loss, and then use the feature similarity of shared modality data to pair remaining modalities from different datasets. } \label{fig:measure_sim}
elements.sent-2411-148: Similarly, when labels serve as the shared modality, we can directly utilize pre-trained large language models (LLMs) to measure the semantic similarity of natural language labels, eliminating the need to train unimodal encoders.
elements.sent-2411-149: In this context, we employ an encoder-only LLM (paraphrase-MiniLM-L6-v2 trained by Sentence-BERT \cite{reimers2019sentence ), which represents input text in a dense vector space for similarity measurement.}
elements.sent-2411-150: In this paper, we primarily focus on cases where labels sufficiently describe the similarity of the data.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, class labels can sometimes fail to fully capture the similarity of an event, leading to incongruent data associations.
elements.sent-2411-152: For example, sensor data labeled identically but collected from different locations (e.g., worn inwards versus outwards) may exhibit significant differences.
elements.sent-2411-153: In such cases, we can enhance the input for language models by incorporating additional context or metadata in structured formats, such as the subjects' gender, environment, and devices used, alongside the class labels.
elements.sent-2411-154: The LLM then identifies the most semantically similar samples and calculates their corresponding similarities based on the language tokens of the labels.
elements.sent-2411-155: After unimodal pre-training, the encoder of the shared modality serves as a benchmark for measuring the similarity between datasets \( D_A \) and \( D_B \).
elements.sent-2411-156: Specifically, the data samples of shared modality from \( D_A \) and \( D_B \) are fed into the feature encoder of \( \mathbf{m_s \).. We have: \begin{equation}h^j_{s} = f_{enc_s}(m^j_{s}), j=1, ..., |D_A|. h^k_s = f_{enc_s}(m^k_s), k=1, ..., |D_B|.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-157: As mentioned earlier, when labels serve as the shared modality, we utilize existing pre-trained LLMs to tokenize the language labels and derive their corresponding embeddings $\mathbf{h ^j_s$ and $\mathbf{h ^k_s$.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Next, we compute the pairwise cosine similarity $a_{jk}$ between features of the shared modality \( \mathbf{h ^j_{s \) from \( D_A \) and \( \mathbf{h ^k_{s \) from \( D_B \), resulting in a similarity matrix \( \mathbf{A \in \mathbb{R ^{|D_A| \times |D_B| \).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Then, \name constructs pseudo-paired multimodal data samples by matching the most similar samples between disparate datasets.
elements.sent-2411-160: For example, for the \( j \)-th data sample in \( D_A \), denoted as \( \mathbf{X ^j_A = (\mathbf{m ^j_1, \mathbf{m ^j_s) \), we search \( D_B \) for the sample with the highest feature similarity in the shared modality \( \mathbf{m _s \), i.e., \begin{equation}p_j = \arg\max_{1 \leq k \leq |D_B|} a_{jk}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-161: Here, \( \arg\max\) denotes the index where \( a_{jk \) achieves its maximum value within the array \( \mathbf{a _j \).}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Therefore, the corresponding paired sample from $D_B$ will be $\mathbf{X ^{p_j _B = (\mathbf{m ^{p_j _2, \mathbf{m ^{p_j _s)$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Moreover, each pseudo-paired multimodal data sample will also be associated with the corresponding similarity measurement $a_{jp_j}$.
elements.sent-2411-164: This process occurs for every $j_{th}$ data sample in $D_A$ and again for every $k_{th}$ data sample in $D_B$: \begin{equation}X^j_{Ap} = (m^j_1, m^j_s, m^{p_j}_2, a_{j{p_j}}), j=1, ..., |D_A| X^k_{Bp} = (m^{p_k}_1, m^k_s, m^{k}_2, a_{{p_k}k}), k=1, ..., |D_B|\end{equation} As the pairing process is repeated for every sample of the two datasets, the combined pseudo-paired dataset $D_P$ will have $|D_A| + |D_B|$ data samples in total, i.e., \begin{equation}D_P: \{s: \mathbf{X}_P\}, \quad \mathbf{X}_P = \{ \mathbf{m}_1, \mathbf{m}_2, \mathbf{m}_s, a\}\end{equation}.

elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}D_P: \{s: X_P\}, \quad X_P = \{ m_1, m_2, m_s, a\}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-165: We now discuss certain considerations before performing data pairing in \name.
elements.sent-2411-167: We can observe that the quality of the pseudo-paired dataset depends heavily on whether features of the shared modality can accurately measure the semantic similarity of different data samples.
elements.sent-2411-168: Therefore, when the disparate datasets $D_A$ and $D_B$ have multiple overlapping modalities, it is crucial to carefully select the shared modality \(\mathbf{m _s\) for data binding.}
elements.sent-2411-169: Here, we summarize two major properties that a good shared modality should possess for data pairing in \name.
elements.sent-2411-170: First, the shared modality itself should be able to effectively distinguish different events in the downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-171: Otherwise, the features generated by the unimodal encoder will fail to differentiate between similar and dissimilar events.
elements.sent-2411-172: For example, in the task of classifying an object's color, mmWave radar is not an appropriate choice for a common modality, as the similarity measured in the latent space would not reflect the object's color.
elements.sent-2411-173: Using this mismatched multimodal data for training is likely to degrade the final performance.
elements.sent-2411-174: Second, the shared modality should effectively generalize across different domains.
elements.sent-2411-175: Data collected by distributed nodes at different times and locations often exhibits significant domain shifts.
elements.sent-2411-176: If the shared modality data is not robust to changing scenarios, the domain gap between disparate datasets will overshadow the similarity of the same events.
elements.sent-2411-177: For example, in activity recognition, skeleton data remains robust across different subjects and environments, making it suitable for data pairing.
elements.sent-2411-178: In contrast, WiFi data is highly sensitive to environmental changes, leading to significant degradation in pairing performance.
elements.sent-2411-179: We explore the choice of the common modality and its impact in Section \ref{sec:exp_select_common_modality .}
elements.sent-2411-181: The above approach can be easily extended to integrate multiple incomplete datasets through successive data binding, even when utilizing different shared modalities.
elements.sent-2411-182: However, while incorporating pseudo-paired data typically enhances performance compared to using no pairings, the improvement does not always correlate with the number of paired samples.
elements.sent-2411-183: When integrating multiple datasets, it is essential to consider their heterogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-184: For instance, there may be substantial differences in data distribution among the paired datasets due to reasons such as variations in sensor placement.
elements.sent-2411-185: We also evaluate and discuss the impact of additional paired data in Section \ref{sec:evaluation-cross-dataset .}
elements.subsection-2411-007: Weighted Contrastive Learning with Heterogeneous Paired Data
elements.sent-2411-186: In the second stage, our goal is to train the feature encoders to learn multimodal joint representations by leveraging both the pseudo-paired data and the original incomplete data.
elements.subsubsection-2411-004: Adaptive Multimodal Learning with Heterogeneous Modality Combinations
elements.sent-2411-187: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:overview , we aim to train a multimodal neural network containing encoders of all modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-188: The aggregated training dataset contains samples with modalities $(\mathbf{m _1, \mathbf{m _s)$, $(\mathbf{m _s, \mathbf{m _2)$, and $(\mathbf{m _1, \mathbf{m _2, \mathbf{m _s)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-189: Samples with a missing modality will include a zero-valued dummy representation such that our training dataset is: \begin{equation}D_S : \{s: \Tilde{\mathbf{X}}_A, \Tilde{\mathbf{X}}_B, \Tilde{\mathbf{X}}_P\},\end{equation} where $\Tilde{\mathbf{X _A = (\mathbf{m _1, \mathbf{0 , \mathbf{m _s)$, $\Tilde{\mathbf{X _B = (\mathbf{0 , \mathbf{m _2, \mathbf{m _s)$, $\Tilde{\mathbf{X _P = (\mathbf{m_1 , \mathbf{m _2, \mathbf{m _s)$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: The advantages of such a multimodal learning architecture are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-191: First, it allows us to fully utilize both original incomplete and pseudo-paired samples, thereby increasing the amount of training data.
elements.sent-2411-192: Although the original datasets $D_A$ and $D_B$ contain only incomplete samples, they are naturally paired and exhibit more ``accurate'' information for cross-modality alignment between $\mathbf{m _s$ and the remaining modalities.}
elements.sent-2411-193: In contrast, while the pseudo-paired dataset $D_P$ may include some noisy, mis-paired samples, it facilitates better alignment across the paired modalities $\mathbf{m _1$ and $\mathbf{m _2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: Second, by increasing the diversity of modality combinations in the training data, the full-modality multimodal model will be more robust to various missing modalities during inference.
elements.sent-2411-195: We also show the deployment robustness of \name on different modality combinations in Section \ref{sec:deployment_robustness .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-005: Weighted Contrastive Learning based on Pairing Similarity
elements.sent-2411-196: Leveraging both pseudo-paired and original incomplete data, our goal is to train feature encoders for different modalities, ensuring that their output embeddings are aligned within a unified feature space.
elements.sent-2411-197: Additionally, it is also crucial to account for diverse levels of similarity among the pseudo-paired samples that arose during the first stage of data pairing.
elements.sent-2411-198: To achieve this, we perform contrastive learning on the unimodal features of different modalities.
elements.sent-2411-199: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:weighted_contarstive , this will push unimodal features within the same multimodal sample closer together in the feature space, while separating unimodal features from different samples.}
elements.sent-2411-200: To accommodate different output embedding sizes from different encoders, we use multi-layer perceptrons to map the features to the same dimension.
elements.sent-2411-201: After applying a normalization to ensure they lie on the unit hyperplane, we have embeddings $\mathbf{z _q \in \mathbb{R ^{F , q= 1,...M$, with $F=128$ in our experiments.}}}
elements.figure-2411-007: \setlength{\abovecaptionskip}{0.cm} \setlength{\belowcaptionskip}{0.cm} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Figures/weighted_contarstive.png} \caption{Weighted contrastive learning with pairing similarity. We weigh the contrastive loss of each sample based on the similarity of data pairs to accommodate different similarities of pseudo-paired samples.} \label{fig:weighted_contarstive}
elements.sent-2411-202: Furthermore, to effectively account for the diverse similarity of paired samples, \name performs weighted contrastive learning on pseudo-paired multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-203: In previous contrastive learning approaches, the positive samples originate from naturally-paired multimodal samples corresponding to the same event.
elements.sent-2411-204: However, the data pairing of \name relies on the feature similarity of the shared modality, which can lead to subpar data pairs, especially when significant domain gaps exist between disparate datasets.
elements.sent-2411-205: To address this challenge, we propose to incorporate the degree of similarity among the positive pairs into contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-206: Specifically, we introduce weights to adjust the contrastive learning loss of each sample based on the similarity of data pairs, thereby accommodating different contributions of various pseudo-paired samples.
elements.sent-2411-207: The loss function for the weighted multimodal contrastive loss is: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{w\_contrast}} = - \sum_{i \in \mathcal{S}} w_{i} \sum_{1\le p, q \le M} \log \frac{\exp(\mathbf{z}^i_q \cdot \mathbf{z}^i_p / \tau)}{\sum_{j\neq i} \exp(\mathbf{z}^i_p \cdot \mathbf{z}^j_q / \tau)}\end{equation} where \((\mathbf{z ^i_q, \mathbf{z ^i_p)\) denotes the positive samples with unimodal features of modality $p$ and $q$ from the same paired sample $\mathbf{X ^i$, while \((\mathbf{z ^i_q, \mathbf{z ^j_p)\) denotes the negative samples with unimodal features from different samples $\mathbf{X ^i$ and $\mathbf{X ^j$.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}L_{w\_contrast} = - \sum_{i \in S} w_{i} \sum_{1\le p, q \le M} \log \frac{\exp(z^i_q \cdot z^i_p / \tau)}{\sum_{j\neq i} \exp(z^i_p \cdot z^j_q / \tau)}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-208: Moreover, \(w_{i}\) represents the weight assigned to the $i_{th}$ paired data sample \( \mathbf{X ^i = (\mathbf{m ^i_1, ..., \mathbf{m ^i_M)\), which is equal to the normalized pairing similarity $a_{i}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-209: Therefore, through weighted multimodal contrastive learning, the model is trained to learn multimodal joint embeddings while emphasizing data samples with higher similarity.
elements.sent-2411-210: This approach helps mitigate the impact of mis-paired samples, improving overall model performance.

elements.subsubsection-2411-010: Sensor data as the binding modality
elements.sent-2411-247: We then evaluate the performance of \name when using sensor data to pair different incomplete datasets for training.
elements.sent-2411-249: For example, on the UTD-MHAD dataset, \name achieves a mean accuracy improvement of 9.82\% and an F1 score improvement of 11.7\% over the best baseline.
elements.sent-2411-250: The lower accuracy of all methods on SUN-RBGD can be attributed to the challenging nature of scene classification from limited high-dimensional data.
elements.sent-2411-251: Moreover, the performance of \name approaches or even surpasses the theoretical upper bound achieved with naturally paired data.
elements.sent-2411-252: We attribute this result to the nature of contrastive pre-training, where training with naturally paired samples may inadvertently capture irrelevant similarities across subjects and environments.
elements.sent-2411-253: In contrast, \name pairs samples from incomplete datasets based on task-relevant features, thereby mitigating the risk of learning confounding features.
elements.sent-2411-254: Additionally, \name outperforms the ImageBind model-binding approach across all datasets, with the exception of comparable performance on SUN-RGBD.
elements.sent-2411-255: We show that with limited data in IoT, the explicit data pairing approach achieves superior performance by directly applying contrastive learning across all the modalities, rather than relying on transitivity to implicitly align embedding spaces in model binding.
elements.sent-2411-256: Table \ref{table:cross_subject_label_bind shows the performance of \name and various baselines when using labels to bind different incomplete data samples.}
elements.sent-2411-257: The results show that \name consistently outperforms other baselines by pairing unimodal data of different modalities using labels.
elements.sent-2411-258: For example, on the MM-Fi dataset, \name outperforms the best baseline by 13.65\% in mean accuracy and 15.2\% in F1 score.
elements.sent-2411-259: Moreover, the performance of \name in label binding meets or exceeds that of using sensor data as the shared modality, suggesting that data labels are generally more effective in assessing the similarity of data samples.

elements.sent-2411-260: In this section, we evaluate the performance of \name in cross-dataset binding.
elements.sent-2411-261: As discussed in Section \ref{sec:motivate_incomplete , we can bind data from two different datasets to enhance the performance on a third distinct dataset (after finetuning on the third).}
elements.sent-2411-262: We evaluate this under two applications.
elements.sent-2411-263: For human activity recognition, we bind data (i.e., (Acc, Gyro, Mag)) from the MotionSense and Shoaib-right datasets, then finetune/test on RealWorld.
elements.sent-2411-264: We also test gesture recognition by binding data (i.e., (Skeleton, Depth, IR)) from GR4DHCI and DHG, then finetune/test on Briareo.
elements.sent-2411-265: Figure \ref{fig:cross-dataset-IMU and \ref{fig:cross-dataset-gesture showcase the performance on RealWorld and Briareo, respectively, with different proportions of finetuning data.}}
elements.sent-2411-266: We observe consistent improvements from adding pseudo-paired data across both label and sensor binding, particularly when finetuning with limited data.
elements.sent-2411-267: For example, on the RealWorld dataset, pre-training the model with pseudo label-paired data samples significantly boosts performance with only 1\% training data (77.9\%), which approaches the performance of using 10\% training data (79.1\%).
elements.subsubsection-2411-013: Impact of pairing more than two datasets.
elements.sent-2411-268: Table \ref{table:pairing_more_dataset illustrates the performance of binding more than two datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-269: While adding pseudo-paired data typically enhances performance compared to using no pairing, the improvement does not always correlate with the number of paired samples.
elements.sent-2411-270: For instance, when incorporating the Shoaib-left dataset, accuracy increases by 2.39\% with an additional 4,500 paired samples.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, adding paired data from the Shaib-wrist dataset results in a slight drop in performance.
elements.sent-2411-272: This may be attributed to the significant differences in data distribution between the Shaib-wrist and Shaib-right datasets, which arise from variations in sensor placement.
elements.sent-2411-273: Therefore, when pairing multiple datasets, it is essential to consider the heterogeneity of the different paired datasets.

elements.sent-2411-327: Advancing multimodal foundation model for IoT.
elements.sent-2411-328: By applying \name for cross-dataset binding, we demonstrate its potential for generating foundational datasets for IoT by effectively leveraging large amounts of incomplete datasets that contain subsets of the required modalities.
elements.sent-2411-329: This approach creates valuable opportunities for training multimodal foundation models in the IoT domain.
elements.sent-2411-330: In this paper, we introduce \name, a new framework for multimodal learning on distributed and heterogeneous IoT data through binding them with the shared modalities.
elements.sent-2411-331: By incorporating diverse modalities like sensor data or labels to bind disparate and incomplete data, \name shows potential for advancing multimodal foundational model training in IoT applications.
elements.sent-2411-332: We also provide insights into the optimal conditions for adopting \name and discuss future directions to further enhance the performance of \name.

elements.sent-2411-340: Most existing multimodal learning approaches heavily rely on training with a large amount of synchronized, complete multimodal data.
elements.sent-2411-341: In this paper, we propose MMBind, a new {data binding approach for multimodal learning on distributed and heterogeneous IoT data.}
elements.sent-2411-342: We also propose a weighted contrastive learning approach to handle domain shifts among disparate data, coupled with an adaptive multimodal learning architecture capable of training models with heterogeneous modality combinations.
elements.sent-2411-343: Evaluations on ten real-world multimodal datasets highlight that MMBind outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under varying degrees of data incompleteness and domain shift, and holds promise for advancing multimodal foundation model training in IoT applications\footnote{The source code is available via https://github.com/nesl/multimodal-bind.. .}
elements.sent-2411-344: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:intro_figure , {\name is the first data binding approach that leverages shared modalities across distributed nodes to creating pseudo-paired multimodal data for model training.}}
elements.sent-2411-345: Unlike existing model-binding approaches, which do not explicitly associate data modalities across different nodes, our data-binding approach demonstrates superior performance on IoT datasets with limited training data and significant domain shifts.
